aliases: Pasta with Pesto, Green Beans and Potatoes

english: Pasta with Pesto, Green Beans and Potatoes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for trofie al pesto alla genovese; closest parent 'pasta food product' (FOODON_03302051) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for trofie al pesto; 'Pasta, cooked' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for trofie al pesto alla genovese as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of trofie al pesto alla genovese is approximately 350 g, comprising cooked trofie pasta, basil pesto sauce, boiled green beans, and diced potatoes.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with pesto sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with component analysis for pesto (basil, pine nuts, olive oil, Parmesan), green beans, and potatoes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for trofie al pesto alla genovese as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted component analysis: cooked trofie pasta (~55% by weight, USDA SR Legacy #20121), basil pesto sauce (~20%, USDA SR Legacy #06164 and component data for basil, pine nuts, olive oil, Parmesan), boiled green beans (~15%, USDA SR Legacy #11053), and boiled potatoes (~10%, USDA SR Legacy #11371). Macros cross-checked against published Italian nutritional literature (CREA — Tabelle di Composizione degli Alimenti, 2019 edition).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked al dente, durum wheat semolina); Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for trofie al pesto alla genovese. GI of 45 is assigned based on the closest analogous food: cooked durum wheat semolina pasta (al dente), which has a published GI of approximately 40–50 (mean ~45) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). The addition of pesto, green beans, and potatoes modestly raises the composite GI; however, the pasta component dominates the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 78.75) / 100 ≈ 35.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 22.5 × 3.5 = 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from pesto sauce and Parmesan cheese
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 735 mg sodium, representing about 32% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt pesto or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from durum wheat pasta and potato content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 45 (low) but a glycemic load of ~35 per standard restaurant serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly monounsaturated fat from olive oil and pine nuts in pesto
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil and pine nut base of the pesto provides a favourable monounsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (~2.5:1 per 100 g), which is consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; saturated fat at 1.8 g per 100 g is within acceptable limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Trofie al Pesto alla Genovese is a classic pasta dish from Liguria, the coastal region of north-western Italy centred on Genoa. It is made with trofie — short, thin, hand-rolled pasta twists made from durum wheat semolina — dressed with traditional Genovese basil pesto (pesto alla genovese), which is prepared by blending fresh Genovese basil leaves, pine nuts, extra-virgin olive oil, Parmesan cheese (Parmigiano-Reggiano), Pecorino cheese, garlic, and salt. The authentic Ligurian preparation also includes boiled green beans (fagiolini) and diced boiled potatoes cooked together with the pasta in the same pot, which is a distinctive regional tradition. The dish is served at room temperature or slightly warm, never hot, to preserve the delicate aroma of the fresh basil pesto. The texture is tender-firm (al dente) with a silky, herbaceous coating. The flavour profile is intensely aromatic, grassy, nutty, and savoury with a mild garlic note. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~185 kcal per 100 g) with a macronutrient profile dominated by complex carbohydrates from pasta and potatoes, healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil and pine nuts, and moderate protein from pasta and Parmesan. The glycemic index is low (~45) due to the al dente durum wheat pasta, though the glycemic load per restaurant serving is moderate (~35) owing to portion size. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan (contains dairy and may contain tree nuts). It is not suitable for those with gluten intolerance or nut allergies. Clinically, the sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, while the low GI and Mediterranean fat profile make it a reasonable choice for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderate portions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in trofie with pesto?
What pasta is used in pesto alla genovese?
Can diabetics eat pasta with pesto?
What are the ingredients in Genovese pesto?
What is the GI of pasta with pesto?
Does trofie alla genovese contain nuts?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, USDA, CREA) contains a direct entry for trofie al pesto alla genovese as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta, pesto components, green beans, and potatoes, cross-checked against CREA Italian food composition tables. GI is assigned by analogy to cooked durum wheat semolina pasta from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single USDA or CREA entry covers this composite dish exactly. Values should be reviewed if a direct laboratory analysis becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or CREA databases for this dish type; the individual contributions from pasta, pesto, and vegetables are negligible and not tabulated in the source databases. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in olive-oil-based pesto and durum wheat pasta per USDA SR Legacy data. 4. The (V) suffix in the input food name indicates a vegetarian variant; is_vegetarian is set to true and is_vegan to false due to Parmesan cheese content. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked durum wheat semolina pasta (al dente) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care); gi_category is 'low' (GI ≤ 55). 6. Serving size of 350 g is a reasonable estimate for a restaurant main-course portion; actual restaurant portion at Ricciotti may vary. 7. Sodium estimate of 210 mg per 100 g reflects typical pesto pasta preparation; restaurant versions may be higher due to added salt in pesto and pasta cooking water.
